comment.body:
I understand you're asking about tariffs between Korea and the US, specifically about a 50% figure you've encountered. You're correct that the US and Korea have a Free Trade Agreement (KORUS FTA) which eliminates tariffs on most goods traded between the two countries.

The 50% figure you mentioned appears to be derived from dividing exports by imports in a tariff announcement, rather than representing an actual tariff rate. This calculation method is interesting and somewhat unusual in trade discussions.

comment.body:
Based on my search, I can explain whether the 50% figure derived from the exports-to-imports ratio would have real effects on trade or be largely cosmetic:

### The Real vs. Cosmetic Impact

The actual tariff situation between the US and South Korea is very different from what might be suggested by the 50% exports-to-imports ratio. South Korea's Industry Ministry has clarified that "under the Korea-US Free Trade Agreement, both Korea and the US have eliminated tariffs on most goods. As of 2024, Korea's effective tariff rate for goods imported from the US is approximately 0.79 percent."

South Korea's finance minister (acting as president) has stated that "the impact of the reciprocal tariff measures may not be huge on our economy, as tariff rates are low due to the Korea-U.S. Free Trade Agreement." However, he noted that monitoring is necessary since the US indicated it would also assess non-tariff barriers.

The newly announced reciprocal tariffs include a 10% minimum tariff on most goods imported to the United States, with higher duties for certain countries. Trading partners are expected to respond with countermeasures, potentially leading to higher prices for many goods.

### What This Means in Practice

The 50% figure derived from dividing exports by imports appears to be more of a rhetorical or political calculation rather than an actual tariff rate that would be applied in practice. Under the KORUS FTA, which entered into force in 2012, Korea and the US have already eliminated duties on nearly 80% of bilateral trade in industrial and consumer goods, with most other duties phased out over 10 years and eliminated as of January 1, 2021.

However, despite the low effective tariff rates under the FTA, South Korea is taking the threat seriously. South Korea's acting President has already ordered emergency support measures for industries and businesses that will be impacted by the US's sweeping tariffs and urged the ministry to closely analyze the details and actively negotiate with Washington to minimize the impact.

South Korea has been actively working to secure exemptions from these new tariffs. The South Korean trade minister has already met with the US Trade Representative and asked for the country to be exempted from reciprocal tariffs.

The real economic impact would depend on whether the US actually imposes new tariffs beyond what's already established in the KORUS FTA, particularly focusing on non-tariff barriers. The 50% figure itself appears to be more of a talking point than a policy that would be directly implemented as a tariff rate.

comment.body:
⸻

What if this isn’t just a negotiation?
   •   Tariffs may not be a bargaining chip — but a core funding mechanism
   •   Signals from Trump, Navarro, Bessent, Lutnick, Miller aren’t subtle
   •   Markets still treating it as noise — missing the shift

⸻

What if tariffs are the new fiscal tool?
   •   $3T annualized deficit requires new revenue streams
   •   Interest on $36T debt compounding fast
   •   Tariffs may now serve as a backdoor tax — politically easier to implement

⸻

What if they didn’t plan this — but are now committed?
   •   Weighted average tariff around 8%
   •   Panic after realizing size of deficit hole
   •   15–20% tariffs on $3.3T in imports likely being modeled
   •   Lower imports = less revenue than estimated

⸻

What if this is economic warfare, not policy debate?
   •   Could be structural, not bluff
   •   Allies’ growth now secondary to U.S. fiscal survival
   •   “Fairness” is cover for emergency taxation
   •   Developed countries negotiated tariffs over decades — U.S. escalating overnight

⸻

What if tax cuts now require tariffs?
   •   “No tax on tips” and similar cuts need funding
   •   Tariffs may be only politically viable tool
   •   Without them, even MAGA tax policies may collapse
   •   Trump doesn’t want reciprocity — he wants tariff revenue

⸻

Summary

This might not be negotiation theater. It may be economic warfare to plug a fiscal black hole. Tariffs are becoming a structural necessity, not a tactical lever. Allies will lose growth. U.S. consumers will pay more. But the alternative — no tax cuts, or a debt spiral — may be politically worse. They’re not hiding the plan. They’re announcing it.

#Tariffs #DebtCrisis #Trump2024 #EconomicPolicy #FiscalReality #NoTaxOnTips #TradeWar #SPX #Macro #Geopolitics

comment.body: Im guessing all those that shorted puts at 5500 were trying to take advantage of the event vol premium and were expecting the 20% across the board (or whatever the base case was). So if we open below 5500 then vanna is actually going to work against that position pretty quickly and there isn't really any put support until 5300. I think BAMLH0A0HYM2 (ticker on tradingview)  is used to measure credit spreads and has been showing a lot of stress

comment.body:
Based on my search, I can explain whether the 50% figure derived from the exports-to-imports ratio would have real effects on trade or be largely cosmetic:

### The Real vs. Cosmetic Impact

The actual tariff situation between the US and South Korea is very different from what might be suggested by the 50% exports-to-imports ratio. South Korea's Industry Ministry has clarified that "under the Korea-US Free Trade Agreement, both Korea and the US have eliminated tariffs on most goods. As of 2024, Korea's effective tariff rate for goods imported from the US is approximately 0.79 percent."

South Korea's finance minister (acting as president) has stated that "the impact of the reciprocal tariff measures may not be huge on our economy, as tariff rates are low due to the Korea-U.S. Free Trade Agreement." However, he noted that monitoring is necessary since the US indicated it would also assess non-tariff barriers.

The newly announced reciprocal tariffs include a 10% minimum tariff on most goods imported to the United States, with higher duties for certain countries. Trading partners are expected to respond with countermeasures, potentially leading to higher prices for many goods.

### What This Means in Practice

The 50% figure derived from dividing exports by imports appears to be more of a rhetorical or political calculation rather than an actual tariff rate that would be applied in practice. Under the KORUS FTA, which entered into force in 2012, Korea and the US have already eliminated duties on nearly 80% of bilateral trade in industrial and consumer goods, with most other duties phased out over 10 years and eliminated as of January 1, 2021.

However, despite the low effective tariff rates under the FTA, South Korea is taking the threat seriously. South Korea's acting President has already ordered emergency support measures for industries and businesses that will be impacted by the US's sweeping tariffs and urged the ministry to closely analyze the details and actively negotiate with Washington to minimize the impact.

South Korea has been actively working to secure exemptions from these new tariffs. The South Korean trade minister has already met with the US Trade Representative and asked for the country to be exempted from reciprocal tariffs.

The real economic impact would depend on whether the US actually imposes new tariffs beyond what's already established in the KORUS FTA, particularly focusing on non-tariff barriers. The 50% figure itself appears to be more of a talking point than a policy that would be directly implemented as a tariff rate.
